content:
Hi all. Here is a branch that contains the rbtree commits I plan to send to the mailing list: https://github.com/matthewtgilbride/linux/commits/rust-next-rbtree

I only plan on sending the top 5 commits there. The 6th is the offset_of macro that has already been sent.

@Wedson Almeida Filho - I'll wait for a reply from you (either here or to my separate email) before sending these as they include your sign-off.

If anyone has comments about them please let me know - I'm expecting some back and forth at least for RBTreeCursor : - )

An initial comment from mine would be to rename RBTree::try_insert to RBTree::try_create_node or RBTree::try_insert_key_val because it'd disambiguate with RBTree::insert as that one inserts an RBTreeNode rather than a key-value pair. On another one, RBTree<K, V> should imply that K: Ord.
Also, some of the doctests in the second patch could still use tree.get(...) next to tree.iter(), that should be considered too.

Martin Rodriguez Reboredo said:

On another one, RBTree<K, V> should imply that K: Ord.

I don't think the bound should be added to the struct, but it might make sense to move it to the impl block to avoid repeating it so much.

Alice Ryhl said:

I don't think the bound should be added to the struct, but it might make sense to move it to the impl block to avoid repeating it so much.

That one is better for sure.

Martin Rodriguez Reboredo said:

Alice Ryhl said:

I don't think the bound should be added to the struct, but it might make sense to move it to the impl block to avoid repeating it so much.

That one is better for sure.

Sounds good to me - will update the patches

Martin Rodriguez Reboredo said:

Also, some of the doctests in the second patch could still use tree.get(...) next to tree.iter(), that should be considered too.

Are you saying to include both? Maybe just in the first example? Something like this:

/// // Check the nodes we just inserted.
/// {
///     assert_eq!(tree.get(&10).unwrap(), &100);
///     ...and so on...
///     let mut iter = tree.iter();
///     assert_eq!(iter.next().unwrap(), (&10, &100));
///     assert_eq!(iter.next().unwrap(), (&20, &200));
///     assert_eq!(iter.next().unwrap(), (&30, &300));
///     assert!(iter.next().is_none());
/// }

Martin Rodriguez Reboredo said:

An initial comment from mine would be to rename RBTree::try_insert to RBTree::try_create_node or RBTree::try_insert_key_val because it'd disambiguate with RBTree::insert as that one inserts an RBTreeNode rather than a key-value pair.

Hm...I'm fine with whatever others think :smile:

My gut reaction is that try_insert_key_val might be verbose if most would understand implicitly that the tree acts as a key/value store. Thus I prefer try_create_node. But if I'm being really pedantic, that name doesn't imply that the node is actually inserted into the tree.

Since try_insert is just a convenience wrapper around try_allocate_node, I guess I'd propose two options:

  1. Remove try_insert altogether. Have clients just use try_allocate_node and insert themselves. Document this pattern.
  2. try_allocate_and_insert

Matt Gilbride said:

Martin Rodriguez Reboredo said:

Also, some of the doctests in the second patch could still use tree.get(...) next to tree.iter(), that should be considered too.

Are you saying to include both? Maybe just in the first example? Something like this:

/// // Check the nodes we just inserted.
/// {
///     assert_eq!(tree.get(&10).unwrap(), &100);
///     ...and so on...
///     let mut iter = tree.iter();
///     assert_eq!(iter.next().unwrap(), (&10, &100));
///     assert_eq!(iter.next().unwrap(), (&20, &200));
///     assert_eq!(iter.next().unwrap(), (&30, &300));
///     assert!(iter.next().is_none());
/// }

Yes, something like that.

Matt Gilbride said:

Martin Rodriguez Reboredo said:

An initial comment from mine would be to rename RBTree::try_insert to RBTree::try_create_node or RBTree::try_insert_key_val because it'd disambiguate with RBTree::insert as that one inserts an RBTreeNode rather than a key-value pair.

Hm...I'm fine with whatever others think :smile:

My gut reaction is that try_insert_key_val might be verbose if most would understand implicitly that the tree acts as a key/value store. Thus I prefer try_create_node. But if I'm being really pedantic, that name doesn't imply that the node is actually inserted into the tree.

Since try_insert is just a convenience wrapper around try_allocate_node, I guess I'd propose two options:

  1. Remove try_insert altogether. Have clients just use try_allocate_node and insert themselves. Document this pattern.
  2. try_allocate_and_insert

The second would if the best of those two in my eyes, though I'd say about an try_create_and_insert as the function creates a node and inserts it.
I've updated the branch per the discussion we've had so far. Please let me know if I've missed anything. Thanks for the feedback!
